prompt:
We're pulling the third-party static middleware out of the appliance gateway (the traversal CVE two weeks ago was the last straw) and replacing it with a core we own. I want it as a pure, socket-free component the gateway calls: build `staticfiles.py` with a class `StaticFiles(root)` (root = docroot path) exposing `handle(method, target, headers=None)` -> a response object with `.status` (int), `.headers` (dict of header-name -> string value), `.body` (bytes). `target` is the raw request-target straight off the request line, still percent-encoded, possibly with ?query or #fragment junk; `headers` is a plain dict whose names should be treated case-insensitively. The gateway does all socket work and trusts what you return, especially Content-Length, for keep-alive framing — so lengths must be exact everywhere: file size on 200, slice size on 206, and on error responses the length of the actual error body (errors are short text/plain bodies).

Path handling is where the CVE lived, so here are the rules and they are not negotiable. Strip everything from the first `?` or `#`. Percent-decode exactly once. Then: any NUL anywhere -> 404. Treat backslashes as separators just like slashes. Drop empty and `.` segments. Reject ANY `..` segment with a 404 — yes, even one like `/assets/../hello.txt` that would technically stay inside the root; we do not normalize our way past dot-dot, we refuse it. Resolution is purely lexical — do not lean on realpath/symlink resolution. The final path must be a regular file under the docroot: directories (including `/` itself), missing files, and every rejected path all get the same plain 404 — no 403s, no redirects, nothing an attacker can use to probe what exists.

Methods: GET and HEAD only; anything else is a 405 with `Allow` set to exactly `GET, HEAD`. HEAD returns exactly the headers GET would (same Content-Length, same validators) with an empty body, and HEAD ignores Range entirely — RFC 7233 says MUST, so we do.

Content-Type from a fixed table — do NOT use the mimetypes module, platform DBs differ and I want deterministic output: .html -> text/html, .txt -> text/plain, .css -> text/css, .js -> text/javascript, .json -> application/json, .png -> image/png, anything else application/octet-stream.

Validators on every 200 and 206: an `ETag` (opaque, double-quoted, stable across requests while the file is untouched, and it MUST change once the file's contents and mtime change) and `Last-Modified` in IMF-fixdate GMT built from the whole-second st_mtime (email.utils.formatdate(..., usegmt=True) produces the right shape). Plain 200s (GET and HEAD) also advertise `Accept-Ranges: bytes`.

Conditional GET, RFC 7232 semantics: when If-None-Match is present, If-Modified-Since is ignored completely — even if INM fails to match and IMS would have said not-modified, you serve the file. If-None-Match is a comma-separated list of entity-tags; `*` matches any existing file; comparison is the weak comparison, i.e. a `W/` prefix is stripped so `W/"x"` matches `"x"`. If-Modified-Since: not modified when the file's mtime is <= the header's date (equal counts as not modified); a date you can't parse means the header isn't there. A 304 carries the ETag, an empty body, NO Content-Length and NO Content-Range. And conditionals are evaluated before Range: a request whose validator matches gets a 304 even if it also sent Range.

Range — single byte-ranges only: `bytes=a-b` (b past EOF clamps to size-1), `bytes=a-` (through end), `bytes=-n` (final n bytes; n >= size means the whole file, still served as a 206). Satisfiable -> 206 with `Content-Range: bytes a-b/size`, Content-Length of the slice, validators still present. `a >= size` -> 416 with `Content-Range: bytes */size`; `bytes=-0` -> 416; any Range at all on an empty file -> 416. Everything else — a > b, garbage, unknown units, multiple ranges — you pretend there was no Range header and send the full 200 (we don't do multipart/byteranges, and RFC says invalid Range headers are ignored, not 4xx'd).

Acceptance contract is test_staticfiles.py — it builds its own docroot under the CWD, pins mtimes, and calls handle() directly. `python3 test_staticfiles.py` must pass. Stdlib only, don't touch the test file.
